pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of data stream processing and median finding. The key signals are the need for efficient insertion and median retrieval as the data stream grows.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Store all numbers in a list and sort it each time to find the median.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list. 2. For each number added, append it to the list. 3. Sort the list. 4. Return the median based on the sorted list.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use two heaps to maintain the lower half and upper half of the numbers, allowing for efficient median retrieval.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Use a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. 2. When adding a number, decide which heap to insert it into. 3. Balance the heaps if necessary. 4. The median can be found based on the sizes of the heaps.

dry_run: Consider the input sequence: [1, 5, 2, 4, 3]. 1 is added to the data stream. Median is 1. 5 is added. The list is now [1, 5]. Median is (1 + 5) / 2 = 3. 2 is added. The list is [1, 2, 5]. Median is 2. 4 is added. The list is [1, 2, 4, 5]. Median is (2 + 4) / 2 = 3. 3 is added. The list is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Median is 3.
edge_cases: Empty input should return an error or a specific value. A single element should return that element as the median. Duplicates should be handled correctly without affecting the median calculation. Large inputs should be tested for performance and memory issues. Overflow should be considered when dealing with large numbers.
common_pitfalls: 1. Not balancing the heaps correctly can lead to incorrect median calculations. 2. Failing to handle the case of even vs odd number of elements can result in wrong median values. 3. Inefficient sorting in the brute force approach can lead to performance issues with large data streams.
